title: Posture Remediation Funnel — Detection-to-Compliant Conversion Rate

compliance.assurance_rationale: PCI 5.2 — anti-malware deployment. Posture validates anti-malware and remediates non-compliance.
PCI 6.3.3 — patch within 1 month. Posture's patch checks evidence ongoing patch cadence.
SI-3 (Malicious Code Protection) — posture validates protections.
A.8.7 (Protection against malware) — posture is one such control.
164.308(a)(5) Security Awareness and Training — including malware protection.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Trigger posture remediation on 10 lab endpoints; 8 succeed, 2 fail. UC-17.1.53 reports the policy with `endpoints=10, compliant=8, conversion_pct=80`.

dataSources: ISE syslog `index=nac sourcetype=cisco:ise:syslog` *Posture* category — codes `15041` (posture compliant), `15042` (posture non-compliant), `15043` (posture pending), `15044` (posture remediation initiated), `15045` (posture remediation completed), `15046` (posture remediation failed). Fields: `endpoint_mac`, `posture_policy`, `failed_condition`, `remediation_action`. CIM mapping: `Vulnerability.severity`.
spl:
index=nac sourcetype="cisco:ise:syslog" MessageCode IN (15042, 15044, 15045, 15046) earliest=-7d
| eval stage = case(MessageCode=="15042", "detected", MessageCode=="15044", "remediation_started", MessageCode=="15045", "remediation_completed", MessageCode=="15046", "remediation_failed")
| stats values(stage) as stages dc(_time) as event_count by endpoint_mac, posture_policy
| eval reached_compliance = if(mvfind(stages, "remediation_completed")>=0, "yes", "no")
| stats count as endpoints, sum(eval(if(reached_compliance=="yes",1,0))) as compliant by posture_policy
| eval conversion_pct = round((compliant/endpoints)*100, 1)
| sort conversion_pct
description: Tracks the posture-remediation funnel: how many endpoints flagged as non-compliant successfully reach a compliant state, broken down per posture policy. Exposes ineffective posture policies (low conversion rate) for tuning.
value: Posture is only valuable if non-compliant endpoints actually become compliant. A posture policy with a 20% conversion rate is largely ineffective — endpoints stay quarantined or fall through default-allow. Tracking the funnel weekly drives policy improvement.
implementation: Confirm *Posture* category at INFO. Schedule weekly summary report (Monday 06:00 UTC). For real-time per-endpoint tracking, schedule a 4h cadence search to populate a summary index `ise_posture_funnel` with per-endpoint state.
visualization: (1) Funnel chart: detected → remediation_started → remediation_completed. (2) Top-N: lowest-conversion-rate posture policies. (3) Trend: weekly conversion rate per policy.
knownFalsePositives: (a) **First-day rollout** — A new posture policy may show low conversion until devices receive the agent and remediation packages. (b) **Endpoint offline** — An endpoint that goes offline mid-remediation appears as non-converted; track only endpoints currently online.
mitreAttack: N/A (posture remediation pipeline — measures policy compliance flow, no adversary technique)
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• ISE Posture configured.
• Posture agent deployed to endpoints.
• `posture_policies.csv` with `posture_policy`, `target_conversion_pct`.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Enable *Posture* category at INFO.

Step 2 — Create the search
Weekly search in `spl` field.

A secondary 4h cadence search populates summary index for trend analysis.

Step 3 — Validate
Provoke as above.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard "ISE — Posture Funnel":
• Funnel chart per policy.
• Top-N lowest conversion.
• Trend over 12 weeks.

Weekly review: posture team reviews lowest-conversion policies and tunes.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **All policies show 0% conversion** — Posture agent not reaching endpoints; cross-ref UC-17.1.54.
• **One policy at 0%** — Remediation action mis-configured.
• **Trend declining** — New endpoint type incompatible with existing remediation.
grandmaExplanation: When ISE finds a sick computer (missing antivirus, missing patch), it tries to fix it before letting it on the network. We measure how many sick computers actually got fixed — the lower the number, the more useless the fix-up rules are.
